name: Beijing
description: Beijing is China's imperial heart — a city where ancient grandeur meets modern ambition. For 500 years it was the seat of power for the Ming and Qing dynasties, and the legacy is written across every neighbourhood: the vast red walls of the Forbidden City, the ceremonial sweep of Tiananmen Square, the quiet canal lanes of the hutong alleyways, and the soaring sections of Great Wall that crown the mountains to the north. Beyond the monuments, Beijing rewards the curious: duck into a siheyuan courtyard, sip jasmine tea in a tucked-away teahouse, or navigate the buzzing night markets of Wangfujing. This is a city that never lets you forget you are standing at the centre of a 5,000-year civilisation.
tagline: The Forbidden City, Great Wall & imperial grandeur

name: Xi'an
description: Xi'an is where Chinese history begins. As Chang'an, it served as the eastern terminus of the Silk Road and the capital of eleven dynasties — the most powerful being the Han and Tang — making it the cultural and political heartland of ancient China for over a millennium. Today it is best known for the astonishing Terracotta Army of Qin Shi Huang, the first emperor who unified China in 221 BC, but there is far more: walk the fully intact Ming city walls at sunset, eat your way through the Muslim Quarter's night market, and explore the Big Wild Goose Pagoda that once housed sutras brought from India by the monk Xuanzang. Xi'an wears its 3,000 years lightly and deliciously.
tagline: Terracotta Warriors & ancient Silk Road gateway

name: Chengdu
description: Chengdu is the undisputed capital of the good life in China. Sichuan's main city is famous first for its giant pandas — the Chengdu Research Base of Giant Panda Breeding gives you a genuinely close encounter with the world's most beloved bears at breakfast time when they are most active. But Chengdu is equally celebrated for its food: the numbing, fiery complexity of Sichuan mala cuisine has conquered the world, and nowhere do you eat it better than here, from hole-in-the-wall mapo tofu joints to the theatrical spectacle of a communal hot-pot dinner. Between meals, wander the ancient Jinli pedestrian street, settle into a bamboo chair in a People's Park teahouse and watch locals play mahjong, or head out to the 2,300-year-old Dujiangyan irrigation system. Chengdu's pace is deliberately unhurried — the locals call it a city that makes you forget to leave.
tagline: Giant pandas, spicy hotpot & tea-house culture

name: Chongqing
description: Chongqing is like no other city in China. Straddling the dramatic confluence of the Yangtze and Jialing rivers, it erupts from the mist in layers: elevated motorways thread through high-rises, cable cars cross gorges, and entire neighbourhoods are stacked vertically up cliffs connected by outdoor escalators. After dark, the city transforms into an electrifying blaze of neon reflected on the water below, earning it the nickname "Mountain City" and "Magic City" in equal measure. The food is fierce — Chongqing hot-pot is oilier, spicier and more pungent than its Chengdu cousin, and locals consume it almost daily. A Yangtze River night cruise reveals the full drama of the skyline, while the ancient hillside town of Ciqikou offers a rare moment of calm amid the vertical chaos.
tagline: Neon mountain city, Yangtze hot-pot & river cruises

name: Zhangjiajie
description: Zhangjiajie is pure spectacle. Deep in western Hunan, thousands of pillar-like sandstone peaks rise vertically from the forest floor, draped in cloud and wrapped in subtropical vegetation — the landscape that directly inspired the floating Hallelujah Mountains of Avatar. The Zhangjiajie National Forest Park, China's first national park (1982), offers days of hiking among the columns, best navigated via the jaw-dropping Tianmen Mountain cable car (the world's longest passenger ropeway), the stomach-flipping glass-bottomed Zhangjiajie Grand Canyon Bridge, and the vertiginous Tianmen Cave — a natural arch at 1,300 m that frames a perfect rectangle of sky. Crowd-free early mornings in the park feel genuinely otherworldly, the peaks emerging and disappearing in ribbons of mist.
tagline: Avatar-inspiring pillar peaks, glass bridges & mountain mist

name: Guilin
description: Guilin has been synonymous with natural beauty in Chinese culture for over a thousand years, and the landscape still earns every poem written about it. The region's karst topography — porous limestone dissolved over millennia into fantastical pinnacles, caves and river valleys — produces scenery that appears almost painted. The classic experience is the Li River cruise from Guilin south to Yangshuo: four hours drifting past mist-wrapped peaks, water buffalo wading at the bank and fishermen wielding trained cormorants on bamboo rafts. Yangshuo itself, a laid-back town encircled by hills, is perfect for cycling the rice-paddy countryside, rock-climbing the karst faces, or taking a slower bamboo punt along the Yulong River. Reed Flute Cave and Elephant Trunk Hill round out the essential highlights near the city itself.
tagline: Karst peaks, Li River drift & Yangshuo countryside

name: Hong Kong
description: Hong Kong is a city of spectacular contrasts — a place where Cantonese tradition and colonial heritage collide with relentless, unapologetic modernity. The harbour view from Victoria Peak, with the towers of Kowloon blazing across the water, is one of the great urban vistas on earth. Down at street level the city is inexhaustibly energetic: follow the Star Ferry across to Kowloon's Temple Street Night Market, lose yourself in the Graham Street wet market, or take the century-old Peak Tram up through the Mid-Levels. The food culture is exceptional — Hong Kong dim sum (yum cha) is a morning ritual, the milk tea is legendary, and the roast goose at Yat Lok is worth planning a trip around. End each evening watching the Symphony of Lights laser show across Victoria Harbour — it never gets old.
tagline: Harbour skyline, dim sum & night markets
